subject: Physics
q: To increase the resonant frequency in series LCR circuit,
question: To increase the resonant frequency in series LCR circuit,
options: Another resistance should be added in series with the first resistance.
Another capacitor should be added in series with the first capacitor
The source frequency should be decreased
solution:
The resonant frequency of an LCR circuit is given by f=12πLC. It does not depend on the source frequency as well as the resistance of the circuit.

Therefore, to increase the resonating frequency product of L and C should decrease.

By joining another capacitor in series, the equivalent capacitor will decrease.

Hence, option C is correct option.

subject: Physics
q: In an a.c. circuit, voltage and current are given by: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> respectively. The average power dissipated in one cycle is :
question: In an a.c. circuit, voltage and current are given by: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>sin</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>)</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>100</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> respectively. The average power dissipated in one cycle is :
solution:
Standard expression of voltage is given by, V=V0sinωt and for current is given by, I=I0sinωt+ϕ. Clearly, ϕ is the phase difference here. From the given expression, ϕ=π3.

Average power dissipated in one cycle is given by,

 Pavg=VrmsIrmscos(ϕ)

=1002×100×10-32×cosπ3

=1042×10-3×12

=104=2.5 W

subject: Physics
q: The RMS value of conduction current in a parallel plate capacitor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The capacity of this capacitor, if it is connected to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> AC supply with an angular frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, will be
question: The RMS value of conduction current in a parallel plate capacitor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>9</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. The capacity of this capacitor, if it is connected to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> AC supply with an angular frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>600</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, will be
solution:
Irms=6.9 μA; Vrms=230 V; ω=600 rad s-1

Current in capacitor Irms=VrmsXC, where, XC is capacitive reactance given by XC=1ωC

Now, current, Irms=Vrms×ωC

So, capacitance is C=IrmsVrmsω=6.9×10-6230×600=50 pF

subject: Physics
q: The alternating current is given by, i=42sin2πTt+10 A. The R.M.S. value of this current is __________ A.
question: The alternating current is given by, i=42sin2πTt+10 A. The R.M.S. value of this current is __________ A.
solution:
The given function of current is, i=42sin2πTt+10 A

It means,  i2=42sin22πtT+100+2042sin2πtT

⇒i2=422+100

RMS value of the current is, irms=i2=121=11 A

subject: Physics
q: For the series $L C R$ circuit connected with $220 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~Hz}$ a.c source as shown in the figure, the power factor is $\frac{\alpha}{10}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: For the series $L C R$ circuit connected with $220 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~Hz}$ a.c source as shown in the figure, the power factor is $\frac{\alpha}{10}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Net reactance: $X = X_C - X_L = 150 - 70 = 80Ω$.
Impedance: $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X^2} = \sqrt{60^2 + 80^2} = \sqrt{10000} = 100Ω$.
Power factor: $\cos\phi = R/Z = 60/100 = 0.6$.
Since power factor is $\alpha/10$, we have $0.6 = \alpha/10$, so $\alpha = 6$

subject: Physics
q: The angular frequency of alternating current in a L-C-R circuit is 100 rad s-1. The components connected are shown in the figure. Find the value of inductance of the coil and capacity of condenser.
question: The angular frequency of alternating current in a L-C-R circuit is 100 rad s-1. The components connected are shown in the figure. Find the value of inductance of the coil and capacity of condenser.
solution:
Current through 60 Ω resistance =1560=14A

thus capacitor current =14 A

∵  VC=I XC

10=14×1ωC

∴ C=140ω=14000=250 μF

Now,

current through 40 Ω resistance =2040=12 A

thus current through inductor =12-14=14 A

VL=IXL=14×ωL

20=14×100×L

⇒L=0.8 H

question: The plot given below is of the average power delivered to an LRC circuit versus frequency. The quality factor of the circuit is :
solution: Quality factor of the circuit $$ =\frac{\omega_0}{\omega_2-\omega_1}=\frac{5}{2.5}=2.0 $$

q: A circuit connected to an ac source of emf <math><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></math> with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> in seconds, gives a phase difference of <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> between the emf <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math> and current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></math> . Which of the following circuits will exhibit this?
question: A circuit connected to an ac source of emf <math><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>100</mn><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></math> with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> in seconds, gives a phase difference of <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> between the emf <math><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math> and current <math><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></math> . Which of the following circuits will exhibit this?
options: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> circuit with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> circuit with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> circuit with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>

<math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> circuit with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math>
solution:
ω=100 rad/sec 

phase difference between e and i is given by 

tanθ=XR           ∴θ=π4

so   tanθ=tanπ4=1

in case of RL circuit  X=XL=ωL

And in case of RC circuit  X=Xc=1ωc

For option A: R=1000 Ω and

             XC=1ωC=11001×10-6=10000 Ω
              tan⁡θ=XCR≠1
For option B: R=103 Ω and

                        XL=Lω=10-3100=0.1 Ω

                       tan⁡θ=XLR≠1
For option  C:R=1000 Ω and

                   XL=ωL=10010=1000 Ω
                    tan⁡θ=XLR≠1
For optionD:R=1 kΩ=1000 Ω and

                XC=1ωC=110010×10-6=1000 Ω
               tan⁡θ=XCR=1;θ=45o

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: In an LCR series circuit, current is maximum at resonance.
Statement II: Current in a purely resistive circuit can never be less than that in a series LCR circuit when connected to same voltage source.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: In an LCR series circuit, current is maximum at resonance.
Statement II: Current in a purely resistive circuit can never be less than that in a series LCR circuit when connected to same voltage source.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct from the options given below:
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
Statement-I $I_m=\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}}$ at resonance $X_L=X_C$ Thus, $I_m=\frac{V_m}{R}$ $\because$ Impendence is minimum therefore $I$ is maximum at resonance.
Statement-II $\mathrm{I}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)$ in purely resistive circuit.

subject: Physics
q: A <math><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> circuit behaves like a clamped harmonic oscillator. Comparing it with a physical spring-mass damped oscillator having damping constant <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi>b</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> the correct equivalence would be:
question: A <math><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> circuit behaves like a clamped harmonic oscillator. Comparing it with a physical spring-mass damped oscillator having damping constant <math><mi>'</mi><msup><mrow><mi>b</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>'</mi></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mi></mi></math> the correct equivalence would be:
solution:
In damped oscillation ma+bv+kx=0

md2xdt2+bdxdt+kx=0…..i
In the circuit
-iR-Ldidt-qc=0
Ld2qdt2+Rdqdt+1c.q=0..ii
Comparing equation (i) and (ii)
m=L,b=R,k=1c

subject: Physics
q: An inductor of reactance $100 \Omega$, a capacitor of reactance $50 \Omega$, and a resistor of resistance $50 \Omega$ are connected in series with an AC source of 10 V , 50 Hz. Average power dissipated by the circuit is ________ W.
question: An inductor of reactance $100 \Omega$, a capacitor of reactance $50 \Omega$, and a resistor of resistance $50 \Omega$ are connected in series with an AC source of 10 V , 50 Hz. Average power dissipated by the circuit is ________ W.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}} \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}} \cos \phi \\ & \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}} \times \frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}}{\mathrm{z}} \times \frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{z}} \\ & \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}^2 \times \frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{z}^2} \\ & \mathrm{z}=\sqrt{\mathrm{R}^2+\left(\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{L}}-\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)^2} \\ & \mathrm{Z}=50 \sqrt{2} \Omega \\ & \mathrm{P}=100 \times \frac{50}{2500 \times 2}=1 \mathrm{~W}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected to a source of alternating current rated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. Find the time taken by the current to change from its maximum value to the rms value :
question: A resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is connected to a source of alternating current rated <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>. Find the time taken by the current to change from its maximum value to the rms value :
solution:
The current in AC is of the form of i=i0sinωt. The maximum current is i0 and rms current is i02. The change in the phase when current change from maximum to rms value is π4 rad. Therefore, the time taken for the same will be,

t=∆ϕω=π4×2π×50=2.5 ms.

subject: Physics
q: A resonance circuit having inductance and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>28</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> respectively oscillates at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> frequency. The value of quality factor of this resonator is_______. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A resonance circuit having inductance and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>28</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> respectively oscillates at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math> frequency. The value of quality factor of this resonator is_______. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
Given: L=2×10-4 H

R=6.28 Ω

f=10 MHz=107 Hz

Since quality factor,

Q=ω0LR=2πfLR

∴  Q=2π×107×2×10-46.28

Q=2×103=2000

subject: Physics
q:
In the circuit shown,

the switch $S_{1}$ is closed at time $t=0$ and the switch $S_{2}$ is kept open. At some later time $\left(\mathrm{t}_{0}\right)$, the switch $\mathrm{S}_{1}$ is opened and $\mathrm{S}_{2}$ is closed. the behaviour of the current I as a function of time ' $\mathrm{t}$ ' is given by:
question:
In the circuit shown,

the switch $S_{1}$ is closed at time $t=0$ and the switch $S_{2}$ is kept open. At some later time $\left(\mathrm{t}_{0}\right)$, the switch $\mathrm{S}_{1}$ is opened and $\mathrm{S}_{2}$ is closed. the behaviour of the current I as a function of time ' $\mathrm{t}$ ' is given by:
solution:
The current will grow for the time $\mathrm{t}=0$ to $\mathrm{t}=\mathrm{t}_{0}$ and after that decay of current takes place.
Growth and decay of current is of exponential nature
$\mathrm{i}=\mathrm{i}_0\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t} / \mathrm{\tau}}\right) \rightarrow \text { during growth }$
$\mathrm{i}=\mathrm{i}_{\max } \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t} / \mathrm{\tau}} \rightarrow \text { during decay }$

subject: Physics
q: In a series LCR resonant circuit, the quality factor is measured as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math>. If the inductance is increased by two fold and resistance is decreased by two fold, then the quality factor after this change will be _______
question: In a series LCR resonant circuit, the quality factor is measured as <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math>. If the inductance is increased by two fold and resistance is decreased by two fold, then the quality factor after this change will be _______
solution:
Q=XLR=ωLR=1LCLR=LRC

Q'=2 LR2C=22Q=22100

=282.84

subject: Physics
q: Consider the LR circuit shown in the figure. If the switch S is closed at t=0 then the amount of charge that passes through the battery between t=0 and t=LR is:
question: Consider the LR circuit shown in the figure. If the switch S is closed at t=0 then the amount of charge that passes through the battery between t=0 and t=LR is:
solution:
The current in the LR circuit at any time t is
I=ER1-e-RtL

Where E, R, L, I are the EMF, resistance, inductance and the current respectively.
∴ Charge flown through the circuit from time 0 to LR is 
(charge) ∆q=∫0LRI.dt=∫0LRER1-e-RtLdt
⇒∆q=ERt+LR e-RtL0LR
⇒∆q=ERLR+LRe-1-LR
⇒∆q=ER×LeR
⇒∆q=ELeR2=EL2.7R2

subject: Physics
q: An electric bulb rated as $100 \mathrm{~W}-220 \mathrm{~V}$ is connected to an ac source of rms voltage 220 V. The peak value of current through the bulb is :
question: An electric bulb rated as $100 \mathrm{~W}-220 \mathrm{~V}$ is connected to an ac source of rms voltage 220 V. The peak value of current through the bulb is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P}=\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}} \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{rms}} \\ & \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{100}{220} \\ & \mathrm{i}_0=\sqrt{2} \mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{rms}}=0.64 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: In a transformer, number of turns in the primary coil are 140 and that in the secondary coil are 280. If current in primary coil is $4 \mathrm{~A}$, then that in the secondary coil is
question: In a transformer, number of turns in the primary coil are 140 and that in the secondary coil are 280. If current in primary coil is $4 \mathrm{~A}$, then that in the secondary coil is
solution: $I_1 N_1=I_2 N_2 \Rightarrow I_2=\frac{4 \times 140}{280}=2 \mathrm{~A}$

subject: Physics
q: An ac source is connected in given series LCR circuit. The rms potential difference across the capacitor of $20 \mu \mathrm{F}$ is _____V.
question: An ac source is connected in given series LCR circuit. The rms potential difference across the capacitor of $20 \mu \mathrm{F}$ is _____V.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}=\omega \mathrm{L}=100 \times 1=100 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{\omega \mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{100 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}=500 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{Z}=\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{L}}-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)^2+\mathrm{R}^2} \\ & \sqrt{(100-500)^2+300^2} \\ & \mathrm{Z}=500 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{i}_{\text {rms }}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\text {rms }}}{\mathrm{Z}}=\frac{50}{500}=0.1 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \text { rms voltage across capacitor } \\ & \mathrm{V}_{\text {rms }}=\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{i}_{\text {rms }} \\ & =500 \times 0.1=50 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The electric current in the circuit is given as $i=i_{\mathrm{o}}(t / T)$. The r.m.s current for the period $t=0$ to $t=T$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: The electric current in the circuit is given as $i=i_{\mathrm{o}}(t / T)$. The r.m.s current for the period $t=0$ to $t=T$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
The RMS current is calculated using $I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T i^2 dt}$.
Given $i = i_0(t/T)$:
$I_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T i_0^2\left(\frac{t}{T}\right)^2 dt} = \sqrt{\frac{i_0^2}{T^3}\int_0^T t^2 dt} = \sqrt{\frac{i_0^2}{T^3} \cdot \frac{T^3}{3}}$
$= \sqrt{\frac{i_0^2}{3}} = \frac{i_0}{\sqrt{3}}$.

subject: Physics
q: An alternating current is given by the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>i</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mi>sin</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>i</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>cos</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math>. The rms current will be:
question: An alternating current is given by the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>i</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>i</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mi>sin</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>i</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>cos</mi><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi></math>. The rms current will be:
solution:
i=i1sinωt+i2sinωt+90

i=i12+i22sinωt+ϕ

irms=i02=i12+i222

subject: Physics
q: In the given circuit, rms value of current Irms through the resistor R is :
question: In the given circuit, rms value of current Irms through the resistor R is :
solution:
Impedance of the given LCR circuit will be, Z=R2+XL-XC2⇒Z=1002+200-1002

=1002Ω

Therefore, RMS current can be written as,

Irms=VrmsZ=20021002

=2 A

subject: Physics
q:
In an ac circuit, the instantaneous current is zero, when the instantaneous voltage is maximum. In this case, the source may be connected to :
A. pure inductor.
B. pure capacitor.
C. pure resistor.
D. combination of an inductor and capacitor.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
In an ac circuit, the instantaneous current is zero, when the instantaneous voltage is maximum. In this case, the source may be connected to :
A. pure inductor.
B. pure capacitor.
C. pure resistor.
D. combination of an inductor and capacitor.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution: This is possible when phase difference is $\frac{\pi}{2}$ between current and voltage so correct answer will be (4)

q: At very high frequencies, the effective impedance of the given circuit will be _________Ω.
question: At very high frequencies, the effective impedance of the given circuit will be _________Ω.
solution:
For ω→∞
1ωC→0⇒ capacitance acts as short circuit
& ωL→∞ Inductance acts as open circuit

Z=1+2×22+2=2Ω

subject: Physics
q: The primary side of a transformer is connected to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> supply. The turn ratio of primary to secondary winding is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math>. Load resistance connected to the secondary side is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>46</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The power consumed in it is:
question: The primary side of a transformer is connected to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>230</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> supply. The turn ratio of primary to secondary winding is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math>. Load resistance connected to the secondary side is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>46</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. The power consumed in it is:
solution:
The voltages associated with the primary and the secondary side of the transformer are related to the turn ratio as follows:

V1 V2=N1 N2   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

230V2=101

V2=23 V

Hence, the power consumed can be calculated as follows

P=V22R=23×2346=11.5 W

subject: Physics
q: An ideal transformer with purely resistive load operates at 12 kV on the primary side. It supplies electrical energy to a number of nearby houses at 120 V. The average rate of energy consumption in the houses served by the transformer is 60 kW. The value of resistive load (Rs) required in the secondary circuit will be_________mΩ.
question: An ideal transformer with purely resistive load operates at 12 kV on the primary side. It supplies electrical energy to a number of nearby houses at 120 V. The average rate of energy consumption in the houses served by the transformer is 60 kW. The value of resistive load (Rs) required in the secondary circuit will be_________mΩ.
solution:
The given data is 

P=60×103 W

Vs=120 V

A transformer has a primary coil and a secondary coil.

The formula for the power delivered is given by 

P=V2sRs

⇒60×103=120×120Rs⇒Rs=120×12060×103=240 mΩ

subject: Physics
q: An alternating emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>440</mn><mi>sin</mi><mn>100</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></math> is applied to a circuit containing an inductance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mi>π</mi></mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>. If an a.c. ammeter is connected in the circuit, its reading will be :
question: An alternating emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>440</mn><mi>sin</mi><mn>100</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></math> is applied to a circuit containing an inductance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mi>π</mi></mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>. If an a.c. ammeter is connected in the circuit, its reading will be :
solution:
Given that E=440sin100πt, L=2πH

Angular frequency of the source is ω=100π rad s-1.

Now the reactance of the inductor will be,

XL=ωL=100π2π=1002 Ω

Therefore, the peak current I0=E0XL=4401002=2.22 A

AC ammeter reads RMS value therefore reading will be Irms 

Irms=I02=2.2 A

subject: Physics
q: In an $L C R$ circuit shown in the following figure, what will be the readings of the voltmeter across the resistor and ammeter if an a.c. source of $220 \mathrm{~V}$ and $100 \mathrm{~Hz}$ is connected to it as shown?
question: In an $L C R$ circuit shown in the following figure, what will be the readings of the voltmeter across the resistor and ammeter if an a.c. source of $220 \mathrm{~V}$ and $100 \mathrm{~Hz}$ is connected to it as shown?
solution:
In case of series RLC circuit, Equation of voltage is given by
$$
\mathrm{V}^2=V_R^2+\left(V_L-V_C^2\right)
$$
Here, $V=220 V ; V_L=V_C=300 \mathrm{~V}$
$$
\therefore V_R=\sqrt{V^2}=220 \mathrm{~V}
$$
Current $i=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{220}{100}=2.2 \mathrm{~A}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Choke coil is simply a coil having a large inductance but a small resistance. Choke coils are used with fluorescent mercury-tube fittings. If household electric power is directly connected to a mercury tube, the tube will be damaged.
Reason (R): By using the choke coil, the voltage across the tube is reduced by a factor \(\left(R / \sqrt{R^2+\omega^2 L^2}\right)\), where \(\omega\) is frequency of the supply across resistor \(R\) and inductor L . If the choke coil were not used, the voltage across the resistor would be the same as the applied voltage.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Choke coil is simply a coil having a large inductance but a small resistance. Choke coils are used with fluorescent mercury-tube fittings. If household electric power is directly connected to a mercury tube, the tube will be damaged.
Reason (R): By using the choke coil, the voltage across the tube is reduced by a factor \(\left(R / \sqrt{R^2+\omega^2 L^2}\right)\), where \(\omega\) is frequency of the supply across resistor \(R\) and inductor L . If the choke coil were not used, the voltage across the resistor would be the same as the applied voltage.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both \((\mathbf{A})\) and \((\mathbf{R})\) are true but \((\mathbf{R})\) is not the correct explanation of \((\mathbf{A})\)
solution: $I=\frac{V}{\sqrt{R^2+\omega^2 L^2}}$ $V_R=\frac{R}{\sqrt{R^2+\omega^2 L^2}} V$

q: When the rms voltages <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>L</mi> </msub> <mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>C</mi> </msub> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>R</mi> </msub> </mrow> </math> are measured respectively across the inductor <math> <mrow> <mi>L</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow> </math> the capacitor <math> <mi>C</mi> </math> and the resistor <math> <mi>R</mi> </math> in a series <math> <mrow> <mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi></mrow> </math> circuit connected to an <math> <mrow> <mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></mrow> </math> source, it is found that the ratio <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>L</mi> </msub> <mo>:</mo><msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>C</mi> </msub> <mo>:</mo><msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>R</mi> </msub> <mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3.</mn></mrow> </math> If the rms voltage of the <math> <mrow> <mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></mrow> </math> source is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>R</mi> </msub> </mrow> </math> is close to :
question: When the rms voltages <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>L</mi> </msub> <mo>,</mo><mtext> </mtext><msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>C</mi> </msub> </mrow> </math> and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>R</mi> </msub> </mrow> </math> are measured respectively across the inductor <math> <mrow> <mi>L</mi><mo>,</mo></mrow> </math> the capacitor <math> <mi>C</mi> </math> and the resistor <math> <mi>R</mi> </math> in a series <math> <mrow> <mi>L</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>R</mi></mrow> </math> circuit connected to an <math> <mrow> <mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></mrow> </math> source, it is found that the ratio <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>L</mi> </msub> <mo>:</mo><msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>C</mi> </msub> <mo>:</mo><msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>R</mi> </msub> <mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3.</mn></mrow> </math> If the rms voltage of the <math> <mrow> <mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></mrow> </math> source is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math> <mrow> <msub> <mi>V</mi> <mi>R</mi> </msub> </mrow> </math> is close to :
solution:
V L : V C : V R =1:2:3

V rms =100 V then V R =?



Let V L =x, then V C =2x and V R =3x

using  V= V R 2 + ( V c − V L ) 2
1 0 0 = 3 x 2 + 2 x - x 2

1 0 0 = 1 0 x 2




    x = 1 0 0 1 0 =10 10

∴    V R = 3 x  = 30 10 ≈90 V

subject: Physics
q: A transmitting station releases waves of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>960</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. A capacitor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>56</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is used in the resonant circuit. The self-inductance of coil necessary for resonance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>. find <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>
question: A transmitting station releases waves of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>960</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. A capacitor of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>56</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi></math> is used in the resonant circuit. The self-inductance of coil necessary for resonance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>. find <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>
solution:
λ=960 m

C=2.56 μF=2.56×10-6 F

c=3×108 m s-1

L=?

Now at resonance, ω0=1LC [Resonant frequency]

2πf0=1LC

On substituting f0=cλ, we have 2πcλ=1LC

On substituting f0=cλ, we have 2πcλ=1LC

Squaring both sides: 4π2c2λ2=1LC

=4×10×3×10829602=1 L×2.56×10-6

⇒1 L=4×10×9×1016×2.56×10-6960×960

⇒L=10×10-8 H

subject: Physics
q: In a LCR circuit capacitance is changed from $C$ to $2 C$. For the resonant frequency to remain unchanged, the inductance should be changed from $L$ to
question: In a LCR circuit capacitance is changed from $C$ to $2 C$. For the resonant frequency to remain unchanged, the inductance should be changed from $L$ to
solution:
$$
\omega_{\text {res }}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}
$$
if $\omega_{\text {res }}$ is to remain same, the product LC should also not change.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{LC}=\mathrm{L}^{\prime} \mathrm{C}^{\prime} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{LC}=\mathrm{L}^{\prime} 2 \mathrm{C} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{L}^{\prime}=\mathrm{L}^2 2
\end{aligned}
$$
